debug_info.goal:
d : ℕ
i : Fin d
μ : Fin d → Measure ℝ
inst✝ : ∀ (i : Fin d), IsProbabilityMeasure (μ i)
f : ℝ → ℝ
hf : Integrable f (μ i)
⊢ ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i

failed_attempts:
have h : (∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ) = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
    -- Use the fact that the integral of f over the product measure is equal to the integral of f over the individual measure μ_i
    calc
      (∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ) = ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ := rfl
      _ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
        -- Use the Fubini-Tonelli theorem to reduce the integral over the product space to an integral over the individual space
        calc
          (∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ) = ∫ x : ℝ, f x ∂μ i := by
            -- Apply the Fubini-Tonelli theorem for the product measure
            rw [MeasureTheory.integral_pi]
            <;> simp_all [Finset.mem_univ, forall_true_iff]
            <;>
            (try simp_all [Integrable]) <;>
            (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_fst]) <;>
            (try simp_all [Function.comp_apply]) <;>
            (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.Measure.map_apply]) <;>
            (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_map]) <;>
            (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_fst]) <;>
            (try norm_num) <;>
            (try aesop)
            <;>
            (try
              {
                apply hf
              })
          _ = ∫ x : ℝ, f x ∂μ i := by rfl
  -- Use the previously derived equality to complete the proof
  exact h

have h1 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
    -- Use the fact that the integral over the product space can be reduced to the integral over the i-th component
    have h2 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
      -- Apply the general theorem for product measures
      rw [MeasureTheory.integral_pi]
      <;> simp_all [hf]
      <;>
      (try
        {
          -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
          have h3 : IsProbabilityMeasure (μ i) := ‹_›
          simp_all [IsProbabilityMeasure]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
              apply hf.integrable
            })
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
          simp_all [hf]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
              apply hf.integrable
            })
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
          simp_all [IsProbabilityMeasure]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              -- Use the fact that each μ i is a probability measure to simplify the integral
              apply hf.integrable
            })
        })
    -- The result follows directly from the general theorem
    exact h2
  -- The final result is obtained by applying the established equality
  exact h1

    have h₂ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by

      have h₃ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
        -- Apply the Fubini-Tonelli theorem to simplify the integral

          _ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
            -- Use the fact that the integral over the product space can be reduced to the integral over the component space
            have h₄ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
              -- Use the property of the product measure to simplify the integral
              calc
                ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ := rfl
                _ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
                  -- Use the fact that the integral over the product space can be reduced to the integral over the component space
                  apply MeasureTheory.integral_congr_ae
                  <;>

                  <;>

      exact h₃
    exact h₂
  exact h₁

have h_main : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
    have h₁ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
      calc
        _ = ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ := rfl
        _ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
          -- Use the fact that the integral over the product space can be reduced to the integral over the relevant coordinate
          have h₂ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (x : ℝ), f x ∂μ i := by
            -- Apply the general theorem for product measures
            classical
            -- Use the fact that the other coordinates integrate to 1
            rw [← MeasureTheory.integral_fst]
            <;> simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_congr_ae]
            <;>
            (try
              {
                -- Ensure that the function is integrable
                apply hf
              })
            <;>
            (try
              {
                -- Use the fact that the other measures are probability measures
                simp_all [IsProbabilityMeasure]
              })
            <;>
            (try
              {
                -- Use the fact that the product of probability measures is 1
                aesop
              })
          -- The result follows directly from the above calculation
          exact h₂
    -- The final result is obtained
    exact h₁
  
  -- The final result is already obtained in h_main
  exact h_main

have h1 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
    -- Use the fact that we can project the integral onto a single coordinate
    have h2 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
      -- Apply the product measure integration rule for projections
      have h3 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
        -- Use the general theorem for integrating over product measures by projecting onto one coordinate
        rw [MeasureTheory.integral_fst]
        <;>
        (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_fst]) <;>
        (try simp_all [Finset.mem_univ]) <;>
        (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.pi_integrable_iff]) <;>
        (try simp_all [Integrable, MeasureTheory.ae_pi, MeasureTheory.ae_prod])
        <;>
        (try aesop)
        <;>
        (try
          {
            exact fun ⟨j, _⟩ => by
              have : IsProbabilityMeasure (μ j) := ‹∀ (i : Fin d), IsProbabilityMeasure (μ i)› j
              exact by
                simpa [isProbabilityMeasure_iff] using this.1
          }
        )
        <;>
        (try
          {
            exact hf
          }
        )
      exact h3
    exact h2
  exact h1

have h : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
    -- Use the fact that the integral over the product measure can be reduced to the integral over the relevant component.
    rw [← MeasureTheory.integral_prod_mk (by
      -- Show that the function is integrable with respect to the product measure.
      apply MeasureTheory.integrable_prod_mk_of_integrable
      <;> exact hf)]
    <;> simp [Function.comp]
    <;> rfl
  exact h

have h₁ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
    -- Use the property of product measures to reduce the integral over the product space to the integral over the i-th component
    have h₂ : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
      -- Apply the general result that the integral of a function of one component in a product measure is the integral with respect to that component's measure
      rw [← MeasureTheory.integral_fst]
      <;>
      (try simp_all [Function.funext_iff, Fin.ext_iff]) <;>
      (try congr 1) <;>
      (try ext a) <;>
      (try simp_all [Fin.val_mk]) <;>
      (try aesop)
      <;>
      (try
        {
          -- Ensure that the function is integrable with respect to the product measure
          have h₃ : Integrable (fun a : Fin d → ℝ => f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
            -- Use the fact that f is integrable with respect to μ i and the product measure is constructed from μ i
            apply MeasureTheory.integrable_fst
            <;> simp_all [Integrable]
            <;> aesop
          simp_all [Integrable]
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          -- Use the fact that the function is integrable to conclude the proof
          simp_all [Integrable]
          <;> aesop
        })
    -- The result follows directly from the above steps
    exact h₂
  -- The final result is obtained by applying the established equality
  exact h₁

have h1 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
    calc
      ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ := rfl
      _ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
        -- Use the property of the product measure to reduce the integral over the product space to an integral over the i-th component
        have h2 : ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
          -- Use the fact that the integral over the product measure can be reduced to the integral over the i-th component
          rw [MeasureTheory.integral_prod_mk_nhds i]
          <;> simp_all [IsProbabilityMeasure]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              aesop
            })
          <;>
          (try
            {
              simp_all [MeasureTheory.IsProbabilityMeasure]
              <;>
              aesop
            })
        exact h2
  exact h1

calc
    _ = ∫ (a : Fin d → ℝ), f (a i) ∂Measure.pi μ := rfl
    _ = ∫ x, f x ∂μ i := by
      -- Use the Fubini-Tonelli theorem to evaluate the integral of f at a_i over the product space
      rw [← MeasureTheory.integral_fst]
      <;>
      simp_all [MeasureTheory.IsProbabilityMeasure, MeasureTheory.volume_simplex,
